title: Chicken korma
subtitle: Mild, creamy almond and coconut chicken curry
excerpt: Chicken pieces cooked in a sauce of onion, ground almonds, coconut milk, and cream with cardamom, cinnamon, and garam masala. The mildest of the British curry-house standards, designed for those who want fragrance and richness rather than heat. Child-friendly and reliable.
sourceNotes: The British curry-house korma is loosely derived from Mughal korma, a slow-braised dish. The British version is far milder and creamier, developed for a market that wanted an accessible, non-spicy curry. It is documented in British-Indian cookbooks from the 1970s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. Reheat gently to prevent the cream splitting.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Good for meal prep.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make 2 days ahead. The flavours improve overnight.

content.text: A korma is built from fragrance rather than heat. The whole spices (cardamom pods, cinnamon stick, cloves) go into the hot oil first and release their aroma before anything else is added. The ground almonds go in with the onion paste and thicken the sauce from within, giving it a silky body that cream alone doesn't achieve. The coconut milk adds sweetness; the cream adds richness. The balance between the two is the main variable to adjust to taste.

Use boneless thighs rather than breast for the forgiving texture that suits a slow-simmered sauce. The chicken can be cooked from raw in the sauce — there is no need to sear it first in this style of curry. Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer after adding the chicken and keep it there, covered, for 25 minutes.

Heat the {{ghee}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{cardamom-green}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, and {{cloves}} and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 15 minutes over medium heat, stirring regularly, until golden. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{turmeric}} and {{almond-flour}} and stir for 1 minute.

Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} pieces and stir to coat. Pour in the {{coconut-milk}} and 100 ml water. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook for 25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Stir in the {{double-cream}} and {{garam-masala}} and season with salt. Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes to thicken slightly. Serve with basmati rice.

items.cause: Insufficient reduction or too much liquid added
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for longer after adding the cream. The ground almonds thicken as they cook — give it time

items.cause: Heat too high when cream was added
items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before adding the cream. If it splits, remove from heat, add a splash of water, and stir vigorously — it often comes back together
The golden onion base is the foundation of the flavour. Take the time to cook it properly. Taste and add salt before serving
Reduce the coconut milk to 150 ml and replace with chicken stock. Add a squeeze of lemon juice at the end to balance

content.text: Korma is the entry point of the British curry-house menu: the one recommended for children, the nervous first-timer, the person who orders it every time because it never lets them down. The British version has drifted far from its Mughal origin — the original korma is a slow-braised dish of marinated meat cooked with yoghurt and whole spices, quite different in technique and texture from the cream-and-coconut version served in UK restaurants. Both are valid; they are simply different dishes that share a name and a lineage. The British version has its own integrity: well-made, it is fragrant, rich, and deeply comforting.
